steps.title: A traffic light at your desk

narration.default: Every junction you've ever crossed has one. Red holds you. Green sends you. Yellow warns you the change is coming. Today you'll build that exact rhythm with three little lights and one Arduino. Before you start, make sure Arduino Uno is selected and you're in Upload mode. If you haven't done so already, finish the Blink an LED and Button-controlled LED tutorials first — this one builds straight on top of them.

Open the Arduino category and drag the setup-and-loop block into the workspace. You've done this twice now — setup runs once when the program starts, loop runs over and over while the Arduino has power. Today setup will configure three pins, and loop will run the traffic-light cycle forever.
Drag a pinMode block into setup. Set pin to 9 and mode to output. Pin 9 will be the red light. Just like before, output means this pin will be sending voltage out — to the red LED in this case.

Drag a second pinMode block below the first. Set pin to 10 and mode to output. Pin 10 will be the yellow light. Same idea as before — we're telling the Arduino that pin 10 is going to send voltage out, this time to the yellow LED.

Drag a third pinMode block. Pin 11, mode output. That's the green light. All three pins are now configured as outputs — the Arduino knows pin 9, pin 10, and pin 11 will all be sending voltage out to LEDs.

Pause for a second. We've told the Arduino about three pins, but we haven't told it anything about when to switch the lights. A real traffic light holds red for a long time, then suddenly it's green, then yellow, then red again. Three lights, one Arduino — how does it know when to change? Watch — you're about to see how.

Drag a digitalWrite block into the loop section. Set pin to 9 and value to high. This is the red phase — 5 volts on pin 9, the red LED lights up. You've used digitalWrite before with the button-LED tutorial; same block, just on a different pin.

Drag two more digitalWrite blocks below the first. Set the second to pin 10, value low. Set the third to pin 11, value low. This forces yellow and green off so only red is showing. Why bother? Because loop runs over and over and we don't want yesterday's state bleeding into today's. Be explicit: red on, yellow off, green off.

Drag a delay block right below the three digitalWrites. Set its value to 5000 — that's 5000 milliseconds, or 5 seconds. delay is what makes a phase actually last. Without it, the Arduino would race through to the next set of digitalWrites in microseconds and you'd never see red at all. The delay is the phase.
Drag three more digitalWrite blocks below the first delay. Pin 9 low (red off), pin 10 low (yellow still off), pin 11 high (green on). Same shape as the red phase, just different pins.
Drag another delay block and set it to 5000 milliseconds. Green also holds for 5 seconds — the delay is what turns one moment of digitalWrite into a whole phase of the cycle.
Drag three more digitalWrites. Pin 9 low (red off), pin 10 high (yellow on), pin 11 low (green off). Yellow on, the others off.
Drag a delay and set it to 2000 milliseconds. Yellow is shorter than red and green because it's only a warning — just two seconds. Same shape as the other phase delays, smaller number.

And there it is — the answer to the mystery from earlier. A traffic light is just three timed phases stacked one after the other. Each phase is a few digitalWrites and a delay. The loop block runs your program forever, so when it finishes the yellow phase, it wraps right back to red and the cycle starts over. Three phases, one loop, forever.

Drag one more delay block at the very bottom of loop and set it to 500. Half a second. This is a habit worth building: every loop body should end with at least a small delay. Without one, the Arduino spins as fast as it possibly can with nothing useful to do — wasted work. Half a second is plenty before the cycle restarts on red.

steps.title: Add the red LED on pin 9

narration.default: Open the Simulator tab. Click the plus button at the bottom-right and choose LED. In the pin dialog, choose pin 9 and click Save. The first virtual LED appears next to the virtual Arduino, wired to the same pin your code is driving high during the red phase.

steps.title: Add the yellow LED on pin 10

narration.default: Click the plus button again, choose LED, set pin to 10, click Save. The second LED joins the first. This is the yellow light — the one that flicks on briefly between green and red as a warning.

steps.title: Add the green LED on pin 11

narration.default: One more time. Plus button, LED, pin 11, Save. Now you have three virtual LEDs sitting side by side — one for each phase your code controls. Three pins, three lights, ready to go.
Click the Start button at the top of the simulator. Your program loads into the virtual Arduino — give it a few seconds. The simulator goes live and the cycle begins.

Watch the lights. Red holds for 5 seconds. Then red goes dark and green comes on for 5 seconds. Then green goes dark and yellow flickers on for 2 seconds. Then back to red, and the whole sequence repeats. The Arduino will keep doing this forever — that's loop, doing exactly what its name says.

Try this: change the red delay to 8000 and the green to 3000 — now it's a busy intersection where the side road barely gets a turn. Or swap which pin lights up in each phase to make a totally different signal pattern. Or add a fourth LED on pin 8 as a pedestrian walk light, and turn it on only during the red phase so people cross when cars are stopped.

This same pattern — timed phases on multiple outputs — runs every traffic junction, every railway signal, every airport runway light, every stop-go robot at a construction site. Christmas-tree light controllers do it. Stage lighting does it. Once you know the shape, you'll spot it everywhere: a list of states, each with a duration, looping forever.

Look what you just built. You drove three separate output pins from one program. You used delay to make each phase last a real, visible amount of time. You stacked the phases inside loop so the Arduino cycles through them forever. And you ended loop with a small delay so it doesn't run hotter than it needs to. Multi-pin output, sequencing, phase-based timing — that's the whole pattern behind a huge family of real devices. You can write them now.
